CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (novel pointer-network approach and joint modeling). Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage thorough, which supports clear exposition; hyperparameters are reported so reproducibility details are addressed. Minor presentation gaps remain (evaluation metrics are not specified in the summary and the effectiveness of figures/tables/notation is not fully documented), preventing an exceptional rating. | B(4): Overall the paper reads well: it has a clear organization from problem motivation to method (modified Pointer Network with BiLSTM encoder and joint loss) to experiments on two corpora and a concise conclusion. The main contributions are stated early and the methodology and training details (including hyperparameters and joint-loss weighting α) are described, which supports reproducibility. Notation and presentation appear consistent and the writing quality is reported as good, though the summary lacks explicit mention of evaluation metrics presentation in the paper itself which slightly reduces clarity for readers checking results. Minor presentation polish could make it exceptional, but it is already well-written and informative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (novel pointer-network approach and joint modeling). Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage thorough, which supports clear exposition; hyperparameters are reported so reproducibility details are addressed. Minor presentation gaps remain (evaluation metrics are not specified in the summary and the effectiveness of figures/tables/notation is not fully documented), preventing an exceptional rating.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper reads well: it has a clear organization from problem motivation to method (modified Pointer Network with BiLSTM encoder and joint loss) to experiments on two corpora and a concise conclusion. The main contributions are stated early and the methodology and training details (including hyperparameters and joint-loss weighting α) are described, which supports reproducibility. Notation and presentation appear consistent and the writing quality is reported as good, though the summary lacks explicit mention of evaluation metrics presentation in the paper itself which slightly reduces clarity for readers checking results. Minor presentation polish could make it exceptional, but it is already well-written and informative.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is a strong empirical contribution that fits the venue: it adapts neural architectures to an important structured NLP task and shows state-of-the-art results on established corpora. Reviewer A’s concerns about formal guarantees and deeper diagnostic analysis are valid but are minor scope issues relative to the clear methodological and empirical fit for a top NLP/ML conference, so I award the top appropriateness score.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP task (argumentation mining) and introduces a novel adaptation of Pointer Networks with joint link/type prediction that achieves state-of-the-art on two standard corpora, so its topic and methods are well within scope for a top NLP/ML venue. However, from a theory-first perspective the work lacks formal guarantees or constrained structured-prediction properties for the produced argument graphs and provides limited ablation/diagnostic analysis beyond empirical gains; these are minor scope concerns for a venue that values methodological rigor. Overall it is a strong empirical contribution that would benefit from more formal/diagnostic treatment but still fits the conference audience and standards.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is a strong fit: it adapts a neural architecture (modified Pointer Network + BiLSTM) to a structured prediction task in argumentation mining, which is precisely the kind of cross-cutting modeling work valued at top NLP/ML venues. It evaluates on two established corpora (Persuasive Essays and Microtexts) and reports state-of-the-art results while demonstrating clear gains from joint link and component-type prediction. The methodology and reported hyperparameters indicate adherence to expected empirical standards, and the task audience (argumentation mining within NLP) matches the venue’s scope.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper offers a meaningful, non-trivial adaptation of known components (Pointer Networks + BiLSTM) to argumentation mining, achieving empirical SOTA and providing a useful joint modeling contribution rather than a brand-new paradigm. Given this moderate but clear novelty and practical impact, a score of 4 reflects that the work is notable and informative to the field while still being an incremental extension of existing methods.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper adapts an existing architecture (Pointer Networks with a BiLSTM encoder and attention) to argumentation mining and proposes a joint loss for AC type and link prediction; this is a useful engineering contribution but not a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. The formulation of link prediction as decoding over input indices is an application of known sequence-to-sequence/pointer ideas rather than a novel theoretical insight, and the summary reports no formal guarantees or structured-prediction constraints to ensure well-formed graphs. The joint training and empirical state-of-the-art on two corpora represent moderate novelty and clear differentiation from prior ILP and MST baselines, but overall the contribution reads as an incremental, non-trivial extension rather than a highly original idea.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper repurposes Pointer Networks with a BiLSTM encoder to address argumentation link extraction and jointly models component type classification — a non-trivial architectural adaptation and the first neural approach reported for this specific task. It convincingly differentiates from prior ILP and MST-based methods and demonstrates empirical SOTA gains on two corpora, which would be informative to experts. Because the core building blocks (Pointer Networks, BiLSTMs, joint loss weighting) are known, the contribution is notable but not a fundamentally new learning paradigm, hence not the highest novelty score.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper compares to a strong and relevant set of baselines on two standard corpora and includes analyses of joint vs. single-task training and hyperparameters, supporting fair head-to-head evaluation. While Reviewer A notes minor omissions in detailed diagnostics and explicit metric-parity reporting across all baselines, these are relatively small transparency issues and do not undermine the thoroughness of the empirical comparison, so I award the highest score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates against multiple meaningful baselines (Stab & Gurevych’s Base classifier and ILP joint model, several Peldszus & Stede systems, a Minimum Spanning Tree parser, and a regular Pointer Network), satisfying the requirement to compare to at least three interpretable/strong baselines. Comparisons appear to be on the same two standard corpora, and the authors report outperforming prior work and the regular Pointer Network while emphasizing the importance of the joint loss, which suggests fair head-to-head evaluation. However, the summary does not indicate detailed diagnostics about when prior methods perform comparably or stronger, nor explicit mention of identical metric reporting across all baselines, so I deduct a bit for minor omissions in comparative analysis and transparency.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates on two established corpora (Persuasive Essays and Microtexts) and compares to a strong set of prior baselines including the ILP joint model (Stab & Gurevych), several Microtext systems (Peldszus & Stede variants), a Minimum Spanning Tree parser, and a regular Pointer Network. Comparisons appear comprehensive and focused on the right competing approaches for link extraction and joint AC classification, and the authors explicitly analyze joint vs single-task training and report hyperparameters, supporting fair replication. From my empirical-methods perspective this constitutes a thorough and appropriate empirical comparison against relevant recent work.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a solid, empirically-driven contribution to argumentation mining by adapting Pointer Networks and achieving state-of-the-art results on two corpora, and that its joint modeling is likely to influence follow-up work. Its impact is limited by lack of formal guarantees, no end-to-end component representation learning, and evaluation on small datasets, so it is not transformative but is a strong, reproducible advance that the community will adopt and extend.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses an important practical task (link extraction and component classification) and demonstrates clear empirical gains by introducing a modified Pointer Network and a joint loss that yields state-of-the-art results on two corpora. However, from a theory-first perspective its impact is limited by the absence of formal guarantees for the structured predictions, no end-to-end learning of component representations, and only empirical (albeit reasonable) evidence on two small datasets that require high dropout. Those limitations mean it is a useful, empirically-driven advance likely to be built upon, but not transformative for how researchers conceptualize argumentation mining.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—automatic argumentation mining link extraction—and convincingly adapts Pointer Networks (with a BiLSTM encoder and joint loss) to this structured prediction task. Empirically it reports state-of-the-art improvements on two independent corpora (Persuasive Essays and Microtexts) and demonstrates clear gains from joint modeling of component types and links, which will influence researchers to apply sequence-to-sequence/attention architectures here. While not radically transformative, the method is a strong, reproducible empirical contribution that opens realistic follow-up work (end-to-end AC representations, segmentation) and is likely to be adopted and extended by the community.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents clear empirical claims that are supported by experiments on two corpora and comparisons to appropriate baselines, demonstrating the effectiveness of the joint Pointer Network approach. However, they also concur that the work lacks some diagnostic ablations, formal guarantees about graph well-formedness, and details on evaluation metrics/statistical significance and code release. Given the solid empirical evidence but incomplete analyses and reproducibility information, a slightly above-mid score of 4 is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main empirical claims (a modified Pointer Network with a BiLSTM encoder and a joint link+type loss yields SOTA on two corpora and improves over a regular Pointer Network) are supported by experiments and baseline comparisons, but the summary shows limited formal analysis: no provable guarantees or constraints are provided to ensure well-formed argument graphs. From a theory-first perspective the work also appears to lack key diagnostic ablations (e.g., encoder ablation and explicit analyses of the pointer mechanism vs. simpler graph-based linkers), and evaluation metrics/statistical testing are not specified in the summary. Overall the experiments indicate effectiveness, but missing theoretical guarantees and incomplete diagnostic analyses motivate a cautious mid-level score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with experiments on two corpora (Persuasive Essays and Microtexts), compares against appropriate baselines (including an ILP joint model, MST parser, and a regular Pointer Network) and reports hyperparameters and joint-training setup, which aligns with my empirical priorities. There are no apparent mathematical derivation issues given the model is an applied modification of Pointer Networks, but the summary lacks details on evaluation metrics and statistical significance testing and code release is not mentioned, limiting reproducibility checks. Overall the methodology is sound and the joint modeling claim is empirically substantiated, but missing evaluation details and significance analysis are minor shortcomings.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents solid empirical validation on two established corpora, compares to reasonable baselines, reports hyperparameters, and candidly notes limitations (data sparsity, non‑end‑to‑end AC learning). The primary weakness is the lack of deeper diagnostic analyses and ablations (encoder variants, loss sensitivity, formal constraints), which prevents a higher score but does not undermine the main empirical contribution; therefore a score of 4 reflects strong experimental work with room for more thorough analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides reasonable empirical evidence (two corpora, multiple baselines including a regular Pointer Network and classic ILP/joint models) and reports hyperparameters, with acknowledged limitations (data sparsity, non‑end‑to‑end AC representations). However, from the summary there are missing critical diagnostic analyses that my theory‑first stance requires: no formal guarantees or constraints on the structured prediction (e.g., well‑formedness of argument graphs), and key ablations are absent or not clearly reported (encoder ablation, pointer mechanism variants, and thorough joint vs. single‑task comparisons beyond the regular PN). Reproducibility is partially supported but code availability is not mentioned, and the lack of deeper theoretical analysis and more exhaustive ablations prevents a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the experimental evaluation is solid: the method is validated on two established corpora (Persuasive Essays and Microtexts) and achieves SOTA vs reasonable baselines including a regular Pointer Network and ILP joint model, supporting the main claims. The paper includes joint-vs-separate comparisons and reports hyperparameters, but appears to lack more extensive ablations/sensitivity analyses (e.g., detailed study of the loss weight α, encoder variants, or end-to-end AC representation learning) that would strengthen causal claims. Related work is reported as thorough and limitations (data size, need for learned AC representations) are acknowledged, and enough implementation detail is given to make reproduction plausible though code is not mentioned.

main_claims: Proposes the first neural network-based approach to argumentation mining focused on extracting links between argument components and classifying component types.
Introduces a modified Pointer Network (with a BiLSTM encoder) used to predict links between argument components by outputting indices over input components.
Presents a joint model that simultaneously predicts argument component types and links, achieving state-of-the-art results on two evaluation corpora and outperforming a regular Pointer Network.
Shows that optimizing both link prediction and component classification tasks jointly is crucial for high performance.
_summary.methodology: Modify a Pointer Network (sequence-to-sequence with attention) using a BiDirectional LSTM encoder and FC layers to represent argument components; set decoding timesteps equal to number of components so each decoder step points to an input index, and jointly train link prediction and AC type classification with a weighted combined loss (hyperparameter α).
limitations: Requires high dropout due to small amounts of training data (especially Microtext corpus).
Does not learn AC representations end-to-end; authors suggest future work to learn AC representations and to incorporate segmentation methods.
_summary.topic_area: Argumentation mining - link extraction and argument component classification

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP researcher with a PhD in computational linguistics and a track record of formal models of discourse structure and provable algorithmic guarantees.
traits: Penalizes papers that provide no theoretical analysis or formal justification for model design (deduct up to 2 points on novelty/insight)
Requires explicit guarantees or provable properties for structured prediction components (e.g., constraints ensuring well-formed argument graphs); deducts if absent

Penalizes empirical-only contributions that lack diagnostic analyses or strong ablation studies (expects at least: encoder ablation, pointer mechanism ablation, and joint loss vs. single-task comparison)
Demands clear comparison to simple, interpretable baselines (e.g., graph-based methods or rule-based linkers) and downgrades if fewer than 3 meaningful baselines are evaluated
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Favors papers that introduce formal insights or analyses; will reward clear theoretical contributions and deep diagnostic experiments, and will be critical of incremental, purely empirical work without such elements.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical Methods Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Experienced applied ML/NLP reviewer focusing on neural architectures and real-world evaluation, with multiple papers on sequence models and argumentation mining.
traits: Rewards papers that adapt neural architectures to new structured prediction tasks (gives +1 to novelty when an architecture is repurposed convincingly)
Values strong empirical performance and robustness: expects evaluation on multiple corpora (>=2) and will upscore when SOTA results are reported with reasonable baselines
Penalizes papers that omit joint modeling experiments demonstrating practical gains (deducts if single-task vs joint-task benefits are not clearly shown)
Prefers reproducibility-minded papers: rewards clear model descriptions and training details (hyperparameters, loss weighting) and will be more positive if such details are present
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic, empirical-first reviewer who emphasizes practical impact and well-executed experiments; enthusiastic about novel applications of neural models that yield clear improvements on benchmarks.
